As wild a finish as we've had in the finale.

Victory!! Fans rejoice as their beloved No Landia take home a second crown (also won in S1)!! The doubters, the haters, the game-wagerers and the naysayers have all been silenced. Their regular season was anything but impressive as NLFY notched their lowest win percentage at an even .500 since Season 2. NLFY only dealt 14.75 damage points per game so their U-DPI (0.074) was less than average. That said, the DNA of this team is not to deal heavy blows, they're content with simply outlasting their opposition. In the playoffs, they swept up their long-time nemesis Smashed Potatoes in three games, then dismantled one-hit-wonder Murphy's Law in 4 games in the semi-final. The home team won every game in the finals series. NLFY and Darwin duked it out for the full seven games. Three games were decided by less than 5 points. A wild finish to the best season yet.

game_commentary.commentary:
Stiffs. Nice win by the new team, relying on a Birds of Paradise, Winter Orb, Entropic Specter and Wormwood Treefolk to take down Murphy's Law. Winter Orb was the winner here, as it ensured that the Law wouldn't summon anything to contend. Meekstone in-hand ready for combat too.

Law. Tough one, they needed several turns to put even three lands in play, and by that time they were on death's doorstep.

The Fault destroyed five lands via Rain of Tears, Stone Rain and Pox. With Pox, they put a Rukh into play who deals most of the remaining damage. One Hymn to Tourach helped out with four damage via Megrim.

FD gets beat here, but it wasn't a hopeless game. They had a ton of mana, put a Mana Flare in play and would have remained competitive if the Fault hadn't destroyed so many of their lands. They handled three opposing creatures with direct damage spells, and generally looked ready to compete. Nice.

Potatoes. Good win against new foe; Icy Hot. They use two Crusade's to pump up a Tundra Wolves, Wild Aesthir, Longbow Archer and Kjeldoran Guard to win the game handily. Two pump ups were huge. Key here, also, was the the Potatoes summoned 10 cards, TEN, with only 3 mana (and help from a Sol Ring). That's impressive.

Hot. Well, they're fun! Pyroclasm is a great card against most teams in this league, and they would have totally thwarted the Potato offense if not for two Crusade's. Will need to learn how to play with them on, mainly, when to do damage and when to kill creatures. Very fun though.

Darwin - Good win here, and a game that they should have won. The game puttered along until the Killer Bees showed up and knocked out the fly-less Attritionists.

Attritionists - three things worked against them in this game; 1) City of Solitude disabled their chance at using Thrull Wizard's counter ability, 2) they had no flying creatures and 3) Hecatomb never showed up. If ANY of those three change, then we might be talking about how The Attritionists won their first game.

MAY have screwed NLFY in this one. Stopped counting Black Vise damage points, and accounted for 1 of them once i realized it, but there may have been one, or maybe even two, more points. My bad :)

NLFY. Junun Efreet sort of screwed them in this game, soaking up two precious mana each turn. Is Efreet's bad worse than their good?

Slivers. Win this one behind Sliver Queen's heft, destroying a Junun Efreet in the process. They could really benefit from Aluren, and I may have to figure out an appropriate trade.

Atts. In a game of defense, the less-offensively oriented won! That's not totally fair, I guess. The Atts won because of a first turn Underworld Dreams who dealt most of the damage in this one. Pushing a mid-game Hecatomb onto the battlefield helped too but was limited by the Winter Orb in play. A good victory, as I wouldn't have played U-world Dreams that early in the past I don't think. They didn't have one creature attack actually. Pretty good. Drawing 8 total lands helped too.

Stiffs. Blew it. They had all their players but were limited by mana (4). Used a Demonic Tutor on a creature, which was foolish (too easily killed). Felt like they were just sitting around waiting for the Demon to get into play and just couldn't get there bc of their own Winter Orb. Lost a defensive battle in this one.

Doom. Smart win in this one but one that ONLY came with the game-MVP Tranquility. They used not one but two of them to dispatch of Hot's Manabarbs and Mudslide in play. The first was countered. An Erhnam Djinn did the rest while a Ants, Ranger and Elf were killed. Drew all three Tranquility cards in this one - lucky!

Hot. Looked to be in control early in the game with ample mana, defense and counters in-hand. They quickly dealt out 6-damage but ran out of answers for Darwin's creature base. Very dependent on the Manabarbs but looks to be a promising series.

Hot. GREAT win! Love the hang tight and wait for the right cards game plan. It really caused Darwin fits in this game. Two Wall of Heat, one Wall of Tears and some counters pretty much nullified everything the Doom wanted to do (attack). Worked very nicely, and Rathi dealt 20 of 21 damage. So fun. Deck might want a creature who can counter though.

Doom. Having no C-Kill hurt them big time here. They had E-Kill and A-Kill but didn't need them. Three walls took them out of their stuff, even after destroying one Rathi. They were completely stalled. Maybe this is the end of all-offense no-defense Darwin..

Stiffs. Wow, another late-game victory for the Stiffs! Their formula for the Atts involved more Mirri and more Storm Seekers combined with game-MVP Orb. Most other cards for them were just noise outside of Tutor's. Two Storm Seeker's were used in this one, with one being used twice (Regrowth) and another being summoned via Demonic Tutor. Mirri wore them down the rest of the way. With this win, the Stiffs advance to the next round!!

Attritionists. Well, this loss marks the end of their inaugural season. They'll be back, as there were a few good pieces that seem foundational for a deck but man do they need help. The defensive play is fine, but they're all over the place. They need to double-down on one or two resources to go after, sharpen the focus and add a bit more offense as well. Their only methods of attack are via Idol's or Hecatomb and the latter is hard to get into play. Changes to come, but a fun deck when in full swing.

Doom. Well done, Doom. They win this one on their terms - in 6-turns. An early Ants led to 15-damage, then a few more came from a live Manabarbs with the finisher via Hurricane. Onto the next round they go!

Hot. Soldevi Excavations backfired in this one nullifying huge counters that could have helped stall the game. Now they have a long offseason to think about the roster, and the Excavations may not be back after this foible. Good run by the Hot and very competitive in this series. Felt like they were finding their strategy.

Darwin. Skated to victory due to Fire Drilled mana issues.

FD. Tough loss due to mana constraints, even after recycling their hand with a Winds of Change.

Stiffs. Win this one partially due to Winter Orb, but also due to poor use of Demonic Tutor. Nonetheless, they ride two Carnophage's to victory, and destroy a Royal Assassin with a Time Bomb to keep the train moving.

NLFY. Bad loss for them, as they pulled a Royal Assassin with their Demonic Tutor instead of a Nevinyrral's Disk - this cost them the game. Otherwise, couldn't get out in front of the Winter Orb to destroy lands, and the Carnophage were too fast.

BAF. Hyppy into Demonic Tutor into Megrim into Pox into Wheel of Fortune. Most all damage was done on turn-6 with their usual combo of total devastation. 

Things. Drew 3 mana in this one, with two destroyed. Otherwise, they may get a Fog Elemental or two on the board, and start their Buried Alive train.

A very, very fun game. I like both of these teams a lot and look forward to figuring out how to play with them.

ML. Were down by 20 to 6 before taking advantage of a Enlightened Tutor-into-Pandemonium with a Phyrexian Dreadnought and a Ball Lightning to deal 24-damage. Yowza, that's a lot.

Attritionists. Have something here. Underworld Dreams did 4-damage, then a few thrulls tried to do the rest. They didn't win but they brought down a beastly foe to 6-life. They also put a Bottomless Pit in play, a fun card. Hecatomb was in-hand but couldn't get it out fast enough, and didn't have the mana to really take advantage. Something here tho...

Slivers. Mana imbalance to start out, then had to be patient before summoning Crystalline Sliver. That was the winning creature.

Hot. Team would much stronger with non-land mana producers. Need to figure that part out.

Doom. Were able to withstand early sludgery of the Stiffs and overcame with their strong stable of creatures. By game's end, a Djinn, Fallow Wurm, Archers, Spider and two Elves were on the battlefield. Two Giant Growth's helped expedite the victory too, which may have been critical in bypassing any additional defense from their opponent.

Stiffs. Well, as good as they looked last round they looked equally lackluster in this one. A much different opponent in terms of build and pace and they were not setup for it. Not nearly enough efficient C-Kill or C-Freeze. Core complaint of this team is how hard it has to work to do what it wants. This was a good example.

Doom. Buh buh bum bum bump, the champ is here! Darwin makes short work of the Stiffs to win the Qualifier Bracket to advance into the Premier bracket as the final seed!! How'd they do it - using a first turn Fastbond to pump out 7 mana in 4-5 turns to overload the Stiffs' Orb. A Djinn and a Ranger did the rest! A critical 1-damage Hurricane was used to dispatch two mana-Birds on the other end, too.

Stiffs. So much work! They used 3 Worldly Tutor's to put two Birds of Paradise in play, then both were poached minutes later. Their third Tutor used on a Walking Wall but it was too late for the speed of Darwin. Thanks for the memories, Stiffs but this the end of the line for your too-slow-for-the-show ways!!

FD. Had five mana, a Fireball, two Winds of Change and a Wheel of Fortune in hand down in the game, and facing a Rathi Dragon. The decision to play Winds of Change, in an effort to gain mana + damage backfired, as it empowered Icy Hot to win.

Hot. Use a few key Counterspell's and a controversial Rathi Dragon to win this one. The Winds of Change gave them a Manabarbs, Twiddle and a few others to seal this one. Rathi Dragon doesn't fit despite winning MVP in this one.

Darwin. Fell down by 12 early due to Spirit of the Night, and would have gone on the offensive if not for the Things countering their Hurricane. 

Things. Offseason roster changes make immediate impact, with two straight Dark Ritual's one for a Buried Alive and the other for a Necromancy to put a Spirit of the Night in play on turn-2. The beast knocked out their opponent 3-turns later. Important to note that the Things won with only 2 mana in play - nice.

Stiffs. Good win for the Stiffs as they remain undefeated. They put a Entropic Specter into play on the third turn thanks to a Birds and Elf. The Specter, with a Instill Energy, is what decimated the Slivers, who struggled to defend without taking damage every turn. A few turns later, a Concordant Crossroads was played with a second Yawgmoth Demon, setting the Slivers ablaze. A Giant Growth to the Elf is what did it.

Slivers. Tough mana game, as they were stuck with only two for most of the game despite a Howling Mine being in play. No way for them to win this one.

Potatoes. Looked to be in complete control in this one. They weren't challenged at all by the Att's. Their Longbow Archer & Kjeldoran Home guard pumped by two Crusade's totally overwhelmed the Att's. They sat on a ton of defense too; Wrath of God & 3 Disenchant's.

BAF. Destroys 3 lands, then discards 5 cards from Murphy's hand. A Demonic Tutor brought a Hidden Horror & Rukh into the battlefield who took care of things in 3-consecutive turns as Murphy failed to draw mana.

Murphy. Only 3 mana in 10 turns is rough. All three were destroyed mid-game, and that left them for dead. No bueno.

Fault. A slow, intentional dismantling of Darwin. Most damage was done by Team Captain; Hypnotic Specter accompanied by a Shocker or two. A Pox made this game appear closer than it actually was. Fault only destroy one land outside of Pox. Megrim helped some as well.

Doom. Lands were all there, but didn't have any firepower to go along with it. With some creatures, this may have gone the other way. Lost a Tranquility early, which hurt them with Megrim on the board.

NLFY. Great win! They destroy 8 creatures and 7 lands in this one to take Game 1! An early Nevinyrral's Disk was critical in knocking out several creatures, a Crusade and a Field of Souls. Then, turns later, a Sengir Vampire was joined by a Demonic Hordes and the table completely turned. The Hordes chipped away at the Potatoes' mana and left them stranded. The Sengir was left unguarded by 4 Potato creatures and did the rest. 

Potatoes. Beaten fair and square despite sitting on 3 Wrath of God's, two Armageddon's and a few other spells. Tough loss, but they'll be back!

Law. Benefit from a great first draw with a Sandstalker, Angelic Renewal, a Tutor, Balance and a few lands. Grabbed a Ball Lightning over the next few turns and used the Balance to knock out a Necrosavant and a Nether Shadow to clear a path to victory. The Ball Lightning came a few turns later once Pandemonium hit, at which point 18-damage was done to lock in Game 1.

Things. Were outdrawn initially, had a lot of lands and Grave-Res and just couldn't stay competitive once the Balance knocked out their creature base.

Drillers. Great win. They matched the Slivers' full stable of creatures with direct damage discarding five creatures in the process. They dealt more damage with a Thunderbolt and Incinerate, then a bit more with a Gargoyle and a Horde once the battlefield was open to seal the deal.

Slivers. This is going to be a tough series. They couldn't get any momentum early and were constantly damaging themselves with mana usage. This is going to be a fun series.

Doom. Put a Djinn out on turn-2 and that fueled the attack all game. A timely Hurricane disposed of two Rukh's and nearly sealed the victory a few turns later. Good win by Darwin.

Fault. Needed some C-Kill in this one to stave off the Djinn attack. With two Rukh's in play and two Shocker's to hold off the Djinn, it felt as tho the momentum had shifted but the Hurricane stomped that out immediately.

NLFY. Incredible win. They were down to 16 to 1 and came alllllll the way back. Phenomenal. They used a last ditch Nevinyrral's Disk to wipe the board, played an Icy Manipulator to control the Potatoes land base, then a Black Vise, Terror, Choking Sands to kill off another land, a Royal and a Demonic Hordes to take the W. Amazing string of cards drawn in this sequence.

Potatoes. This one just got away. They were cooking early with attackers and C-Pump on the board. A Cyclopean Tomb turned four lands into Swamps and four more were destroyed. Tough loss for the Potatoes.

Law. Won this game with only two Mountain's for most of the game. A Sol Ring enabled all but 3 damage via the game MVP-Sandstalker. A Ball Lightning helped too, as did the game-ending Lightning Bolt. Out-drew their opponent in this one.

Things. Started with a lot of mana, two Kraken's and a Deep Spawn. Just a clunky mix that didn't go anywhere.

Things. Back to the basic formula, the Things lay a first-turn Buried Alive and follow it up with a Living Death a few turns later. Then, a whopping 32-damage rained down on the Law via two Kraken's, a Fog Elemental and team captain; Spirit of the Night.

Law. Had nothing here, and showed the true colors that were evident all season long. Mana struggles into no offense or defense. There's just no way this team can slow anyone down - it's their way or their doors get blown off. Not sustainable strategy.

Doom. Well, well!! The Doom are on the offensive against the #1-seed! This time, they relied heavily on a Fyndhorn Elder to launch their attack - an Ants/Archer combo did most of the damage, with a Hurricane to help finish it off. Very, very nice win by the former #1-seed.

Fault. Find themselves in odd territory down 2 games to 1. They had mana struggles early, and that stunted their defensive onslaught. A late Wheel of Fortune did little to help, and they were left for dead quickly by turn 5. Ut oh.

Drillers. Great win! They were patient and demonstrative, throwing down a 12-damage turn with two Chain Lightning's, a Lightning Bolt and a Fork. An earlier Ball Lightning and an Incinerate later, and this one was over. Very fun to play with this much firepower. Two Shivan's in-hand were insurance :)

Slivers. Bonnuit, Lining. That's the end of the season at the hands of a sweep. They had three mana in play but none produced the needed Forest to put a Muscle and a Victual Slivers in play. The early Crystalline was very helpful though.

NLFY. Redemption!!! No Landia wins this in a romp! The twin Black Vise's dealt 20 damage in 4 turns as the former champ struggled to put enough mana in play to start under the Vise's hand tax. Highly unusual for NLFY to win in this fashion but onto the next round!

Potatoes. They are stunned. The former champ brushed away the first loss in this series, grew a slight concern after the second loss and are downright speechless after this one. They were beaten to the punch by a notoriously slower team and will have an entire off season to think about it. Disappointing for the Potatoes.

Law. Saved by Pandemonium to win the game!! The Things had just played a Spirit of the Night & Nether Shadow and looked to be in control. Drawing the MVP enabled not one but two 'Nought's to enter the battlefield, doling out 24 damage in the process. HUGE win to propel the Law past the Things and into the next round!!!

Things. What happened...Well, their reliance on a 1-2 punch of Buried Alive into Living Death wasn't there in 3 of these games. The only time it showed up, Game 3, ended in victory. This team suddenly appears too vulnerable, and dependent, falling prey to a lowly foe like the Law. Need to look into complementary adjustments to allow for more defense.

Doom. The impossible made possible. Darwin skates on by thanks largely to a Sylvan Library, doling out muchos damage via a Ranger/Ants combo. A Tranquility saved them the game, as it destroyed two Megrim's in play with a certain Pox to follow. See ya, Fault!

Fault. May have been misplayed here, but the game was in-hand with the double-Megrim in play and a Pox to follow. Earlier Wheel of Fortune could have hurt them, though unclear.

Drillers. Two Mana Flare's give them 15 mana to work with. Unfortunately, they were low on direct damage spells. A Kaervek's Torch, Lightning Bolt, and an Incinerate later, and this game was over. 

Stiffs. Sitting on 9-mana with very little to summon is highly unusual. FS was hoping for a Meekstone and/or a Winter Orb to stop the madness, but never pulled either card.

Attritionists. Well that was awesome. Their full force sans Hecatomb came out on display in this one, as they totally wore down their opponent with Death Pits of Rath and Jinxed Idol to eliminate the creature base, Desolation to remove lands, Bottomless Pit for hand removal and finally, The Rack for some additional damage. They played only 2 creatures in this game. What fun.

Slivers. This is a tough team for them to beat, as shown in their last two meetings (0-2). With some plains/swamp help they may have changed their fate, but unlikely.

NLFY. Well, the hung in there, but never recovered after BAF beat them to the punch with L-Kill cards. They fell behind, and had no offense for the rest of the game. BAF killed off 8 total lands.

BAF. Jumped the L-Kill game due to turn-2 Sol Ring, played a Pox on turn-15 to shake things up a bit, and were able to survive Nevinyrral's Disk to put a Hidden Horror / Rukh combo in play for the win.

Potatoes. Were the constant aggressor in this one, but sealed their victory with a late-game Wrath of God. They had a bunch of creatures, help from a Crusade, and were able to defend against several creatures and enchantments from Darwin.

Darwin. Well, this is interesting. They withstood a quick attack from the Potatoes, then clearing the opposing attackers with a Theolnite Druid and Killer Bees in play to do the rest, but Wrath of God eliminated their chance to do combat. Tough loss for Darwin.

Things. An unconventional win here, taking full advantage of MVP Dark Ritual in their first turn to put a Hidden Horror in play, following it with a Fog Elemental two turns later. Easy win for the Things in five turns.

Law. Nothing here, again. The imbalance of their mana has struck yet another time. Might need to tweak the balance here some more.

Drillers. Long one, but win at the hands of two Balduvian Horde and 12 direct damage cards. They killed 9 opposing creatures in the process - reminiscent of their series against the Slivers. Great win.

Doom. Ut oh, not good. Pulling nine creatures with all of them ending up in the graveyard is pretty tough. Their only way to do damage is via creatures and if most of them are being destroyed immediately, then it's hard to see a path to victory. Could be the undoing of Darwin's run.

NLFY. In complete control the entire game. Several M-Kill cards put them in the driver seat early, and then a Icy helped immobilize the Law. A Sengir Vampire dealt most of the non-Black Vise damage, and was followed by a Demonic Hordes and another Sengir. No chance once the mana imbalance settled.

Law. Couldn't ever get anything going in this game. Fell victim to low mana base + Black Vise, and really didn't recover at all. This group needs all the stars to align in order for them to win, and they had nothing going in this one.

Law. Great win! They dodge a bullet by losing only one land in this game. Five other lands produce two Pandemonium cards and a game ending Nought. Seven other damage done by a Sandstalker and a lightning bolt.

NLFY. Not a game they could've won, I don't think. Early Black Vise helped them secure a lead but their usual offense-by-defense wasn't there.

Doom. Good win. The Sylvan Library helped big time and enabled much mana acceleration to put them out front. Damage was dealt by a pumped up Ants, a pumped up Theolite Druid and two Hurricane's (one killed a Shivan). Nice win and nicer control from the outset.

Drillers. Were stuck early on with low mana, but used six direct damage cards to take out opposing creatures. They had one Horde and one Shivan destroyed which was totally game-changing. Sat on two Furnace of Rath cards and didn't quite see a alley to play them. Same with Granite Gargoyle's.

NLFY. Grinding grinding grinding away. The all-blacks take down the Law with some clean defense and then ride a Demonic Hordes to victory. 

Law. Just didn't have enough lands in this - shocking. Only had 4 all game, and all were destroyed. They left game-winning combo in their hand stranded. Need more out of this team if they're going to contend!!!

Doom. Extremely well timed Hurricane gives the Doom the win. They lost six creatures in this game but held on with a Druid and a Wurm. Only four lands too.

Drillers. Felt behind this whole game despite having home court advantage. Losing a Horde and Shivan was critical.

NLFY. Great win. They looked behind from the initial draw, but were able to play sound defense thanks to the game MVP-Icy. They also dispatched 5 critical lands to freeze up the Law. A Sengir Vampire, Royal and Black Vise took care of the rest.

Law. Real tough loss. The winning combo was in the hand from the start but they couldn't get to 4-mana to summon a Pandemonium. They had a chance to level the game with a Wheel of Fortune but it didn't get them all the way there.

FD. Big comeback pushes this series back to even. They were down to 3 life, and had only dealt 6-damage themselves until two Shivan's and 7 lands put the game out of reach. Amassing Mountain's was critical in their victory to fuel the twin Dragon's.

Doom. So so close. They lost 5 creatures in this game due to direct damage and still had more offense to unleash. A Yavimaya Ants did serious damage, as did a Thelonite Druid ability. Two Djinn's in hand were somewhat useless against the double Dragon attack.

Drillers. Well well, old friend Furance of Rath sets the stage for 20 pts of damage in last turn of the game; 4 from Gargoyle's, then combined 16 from a Thunderbolt, Incinerate and Shock. Just in case, they were sitting on a Shivan with two more Mountains on the way. 

Doom. One more turn has them sending 20 damage via 5 Forest's courtesy of the Furnace. Didn't get there - game over.

NLFY. Two Black Vise's wreak havoc on the soon-to-be-former Murphy's Law. All damage was done by the twin Vise's except for a Choking Sands dealing two on a Plateau. NLFY won this series in a few different ways, which is always fun to see. They were able to win this game despite only having 3 Swamps in play.

Law. Combine mana issues with Black Vise's and you're toast. A dearth of defense completely disabled any chance they could win this game. Bigger issues despite a first round victory against fellow-combo-dependent; Things.

Doom. Huge victory thanks to big boy Force of Nature. Joined by a Yavimaya Ants, the Doom dealt eight damage in the final turn to win the game all because of trample. Nice. Earlier damage done by a Wurm, and an Archer.

Drillers. Had to use more direct damage than anticipated to kill off bigger creatures like the Force and the Fallow Wurm. No offense in this game.

Doom. Ah ha!!!!!!! There it is, a game 7 victory propelling the Qualifier Bracket champion to the Premier League finale. What a road! They won this thanks to Sylvan Library - picking off card by card to surge ahead - then a Force of Nature who just couldn't be taken down but the Drillers. A ton of mana helped tremendously. Great victory!

Drillers. What a disappointing way to go out - stuck on three mana to go down without much of a fight. They were dominated in this one, taking twenty damage via the Force and a Thelonite Druid. See ya, Drillers.

Murphy. Pandemonium enabled an early Phyrexian Dreadnought attack, then a 4th land on turn-7 let loose the Thundermare for the final ten damage. Glad to see some of their strengths come together finally.

NLFY. Sinkhole! Played on the second turn - how fun! Had Nevinyrral's Disk been played earlier, it would've been destroyed by Disenchant. Wasn't enough M-Kill early on to disable Murphys Momentum.

FD. Beneficiaries of two Mana Flare's that powered a 15-land Fireball to end the game. Power Surge made it onto the battlefield in this one as well, and was interesting. This might be more of a 'loss' for the Potatoes tho...

Potatoes. Sitting pretty with four creatures in play backfired, as they should've saved their last three mana for a Reverse Damage instead of putting a White Knight in play. Chalk this one up to 'user error'.

Darwin. Totally stifled by lack of mana + Dream Tides. The mana enabled them to summon 8 creatures, which is normally enough to take down a creature-less opponent, but the Dream Tides disabled their ability to untap at all. So, one attack per creature. Eeeeks. 

Icy Hot. HUGE win by the Hot! They rely entirely on Dream Tides in this one, stave off any potential issues (Force of Nature, Thelonite Druid) and take out Darwin with a Rathi Dragon. NIce!!

Slivers. Four slivers on the board, and all deposited into the graveyard by Living Death while well on their way to victory - the same old song.

Things. A Dark Ritual into Living Death has been devastating for opponents. In this one, the Things put a Polar Kraken (due to discard) and a Baron Sengir (due to Hidden Horror) into play for two turns of mayhem. Night night, Slivers.

Fault. Winning in 5-turns is almost unfair. This time, they used two M-Kill cards to stymie any momentum for the Stiffs, then capitalized on a Megrim w/ a Shocker and a Pox to win the game. 

Stiffs. What can you do in 4-turns? All 4 mana producers were killed, they took a hit on the Shocker to get a new hand, which backfired because they let go of a Tranquility, which could have saved them.

NLFY. What a game, coming down to a single point of life for the all-blacks. Two Terror's were absolutely critical to the win dispatching of an early Elder and a Fallow Wurm - both gave NLFY time to put some offense on the board in Sengir Vampire.

Doom. So close. They withstood more defense than maybe ever in their teams' history, losing 7 lands and 8 creatures. Just an absurd deficit to overcome. This is going to be a great series.

Doom. Skin of their teeth, the Doom take Game 2 thanks to a game-changing impact by City of Solitude. The City halted NLFYs ability to play defense with Icy Manipulator. A Force of Nature joined a Wurm, and they closed things out quickly.

NLFY. Drew a second swamp on turn-8, a tough draw for a roster of cards with BB casting costs. They destroyed only two lands and were left vulnerable for much of the game.

NLFY. Home team wins for the third time in a row this series. A first-turn Black Vise dealt 9 damage fast, then a second Vise helped end it two turns later. Two Sinkehole's put the hurt on and guaranteed victory.

Doom. No chance to get out in front of the M-Kill without an Elf or Elder.

Doom. Home field advantage helped them put a turn-2 Aluren in play, which also meant an Archers and a Wurm. The offense was on early for Darwin. They subsided on these two to take out NLFY a few turns later.

NLFY. Only one M-Kill card early, and it wasn't fast enough to disrupt Darwin's speed. They also didn't have enough C-Kill - one Hellfire but it was too late to summon.

NLFY. Wow, they were down to 1pt after the 7th turn. Then, the MVP Royal started to do work against a strong Doom creature offensive, killing 3 in the process as they attacked relentlessly. A Black Vise helped early on, and a Terror was key to dispatch a Djinn too. Huge comeback win.

Doom. Well that might be the series - had the home team down big and just couldn't finish the job. One more turn gets them the W, but it wasn't there.

Doom. Good rebuttal by the Doom. In their 15 drawn cards, they had 8 lands -- huge against a M-Kill team like NLFY. Not only that, but their seven other cards included two Giant Growth's for an Archer, an Ants and a game-ending Hurricane. Niceeeeee.

NLFY. No chance in this one. Darwin simply outdrew them. Mana struggles didn't help either.

NLFY. In what looked to be No Landia's undoing, they drew two Black Vise's early and were able to subside on only one land for the first four turns. They used a Dark Ritual to destroy an Forest and killed an Elvish Archers with a Terror. Annnnnnnd that's it! Fastest game possible for this team in a Game 7 clincher!!

Doom. Disappointing way to go out - mana strapped against the most devastating M-Kill team in the league. No one wins this game against NLFY, but an admirable run by the Qualifier Bracket winner.

Sliver. This is the Sliver Team we're used to - 6 different slivers on the board at one time, with a finishing move of Coat of Arms. Yow!

Darwin. Three lands with only two creatures is a recipe for disaster against the slivers.

NLFY. WHAT A GAME!!!! A total of 15 turns to decide this in favor of No Landia. Would LOVE to go into the details, but my better half is demanding I get ready for dinner. Outrageous, I know. Anyway, they rely on killing off 8 mana, a game-saving Icy Manipulator to stall a Spirit of the Night for a ridiculous comeback victory. Awesome game.

Things. Had this in the bag with their near-unstoppable Spirit of the Night until NFLY drew the only card that could have saved them. Tough loss.

Law. HELL YEAH! The architecture finally came to fruition in this one in a wild finish against the defending-champ. Four turns in, the Law had a Pandemonium on the board and little else. The Potatoes played an Armageddon thinking it'd be the only way to stop whatever madness was to come. In three successive turns, the Law drew a Plains, a Sol Ring and a Mountain. Those four mana gave way to a Lava Hounds, another Pandemonium, a Angelic Renewal to set them up for another creature. On their 9th turn, they drew the monster of all monsters in Phyrexian Dreadnought and played him for 48 total damage (2 Pandemonium + Angelic Renewal) - the most damage ever done in a single turn and knocking the Potatoes back to a historic -40 poitns. Ay carumba, what a win.

Potatoes. A Tundra Wolves, Wild Aesthir and a Sol Ring in played meant their Armageddon on turn-4 made perfect sense. They didn't plan on the bounce-back from Murphys Law, or going land-less in their next 5 turns. Tough loss.

Stiffs. Fascinating game. A Winter Orb made it out early, stifling The Attritionists' chance at maximizing Hecatomb. Sealing the deal, though, was actually newcomer Lake of the Dead, who popped onto the battlefield, took advantage of it's abilities and plopped a Yawgmoth Demon right into play. Two turns later, this one was over. 

Attritionists. Likely win this game without Winter Orb on the board. Had strong mana (6), a Hecatomb and Death Pits of Rath in their hand for extra coverage.

Potatoes. Beats NLFY by Armageddon - a trend with these two. Still, No Landia's response to White Knight with Icy Manipulator was helpful, along with a Royal to threatened destruction for any other creature. A Crusade put their two Soul Warden's, Knight and Tundra Wolves over the top for the win.

NLFY. They're getting there. This loss marked progress against a team that has absolutely had their number in recent match-ups. Feels like some additional creatures would be helpful, and I think I know just the thing..

Icy. Wins this one on the heels of two Pyrokinesis cards. The first destroyed a Hidden Horror, and the second won the game. Playing Mana Barbs early totally changed the way the game was played and, really, Icy Hot could just sit and wait for the Pyro to be drawn while the Things hurt themselves turn after turn.

Things. Couldn't draw the necessary 2MC Grave-Res cards to put a monster onto the battlefield. Instead, they continually hurt themselves by Mana Flare. Showed the need for some H-Pump in the form of a Demonic Tutor..

Drillers. Good win for the Drillers here. They destroyed four opposing creatures early, then leveraged a MVP Mana Flare to fuel a Shivan, Gargoyle, and Fireball's to seal the deal. A Power Surge was helpful too.

Attritionists. Landed most of their foundation, and even saw three Sengir Autocrat's hit the battlefield. Unfortunately, there was no Hecatomb in sight, so they were torn to shreds with the superior offense from FD. A Desolation helped dim some of the pain, but they couldn't last.
Somewhat uneventful game where Darwin raced out with offense faster than the Frozen Stiffs could summon defense. A game Darwin needed to win, and did.

Slivers. Pump out 7 creatures quickly and win this game in their next turn if not for Final Fortune.

Murphy's Law. ONE plains spells a world of hurt for the Slivers here. A Pandemonium, a Dreadnought, two Enlightened Tutor's...one more plains.

Drillers. Starting to think they'll never beat a team like the Things. There's just no way for them to deal with the sheer heft of the Things' creature base. The best they can do is hope for mana struggles or a faster start to the game. 

Things. Use a Dark Ritual to summon Living Death and bring a Polar Kraken into play on the fifth turn. Two plays later, and a Fog Elemental and Nether Shadow joined in on the fun to -11.

Icy Hot. Another victory for the Hot! This time, they ride an early Rathi Dragon to deal the first ten damage, then sit on some direct damage to do some more, then a second Manabarbs (first one was destroyed) to finish them off. Worth noting that Maze of Ith was extremely helpful in this game, keeping any single creature attack from the Stiffs completely at bay.

Stiffs. Interesting game for them. They froze the board early with a Winter Orb and Meekstone, then struggled to put any offense in play. A Titania's Song could've enlivened five artifacts, but it would've freed the Rathi in the process. One C-Kill could've changed that, but it never came.

Attritionists. Interesting win against their fellow bottom-feeder. They use a first turn Bottomless Pit to rid NLFY of their hand, including a Sengir Vampire, Black Knight and Demonic Horde. It did the same to their own hand, but it didn't matter at all. They could sit by waiting for The Rack and Jinxed Idol to clean up the mess. Great win by them, and makes me think Bottomless Pit should be more of a factor.

NLFY. Tough loss here. They drew more than half their total lands in this game, but only 3 creatures with offense. They could have summoned ANYTHING, and were eventually done-in by the Jinxed Idol. Too bad, I was excited to see them win one with their new FAs.

Darwin. Wow, nice 5-turn win by the Doom. They leveraged Fyndhorn Elder for some serious speed here, who helped put out an Elvish Archers, a Thelonite Druid and Y-Ants. That alone led to 10-damage in one turn, then another 11-damage the next to finish off the Law. Nice.

Murphy's Law. Two turns away from victory, they sat with three lands on the board, two more coming up and a hand full'a mayhem. Tough loss.

Potatoes. Ride a Crusade, a Icatian Town and a Kjeldoran Outpost to victory. Land Tax helped also. Little resistance from the Fault in this one. 

Fault. Not nearly enough defense to withstand the quick attack from the Potatoes.

Potatoes. Barely scraped by for the win thanks to a game-saving Wrath of God. They had four creatures on the battlefield, two of them frozen, and were facing three bruisers when the WoG came. It enabled the Potatoes a free path to victory two turns later with a Icatian Town. Two Crusades, a Land Tax, a Zuran Orb were all instrumental in this victory. Not sure they could've prevailed without those cards.

Stiffs. One card away from winning. If not for the WoG, they attack with a 7/7 Entropic Specter, a Yawgmoth Demon and a Llanowar Elf for the win. Great to see their core dynamics in play with Meekstone, Winter Orb, Instill Energy all contributing to the effort in a nice way. Great promise for this team.

Drillers. Nice, a new strategy coming together in this one with Urzatron + Mana Flare to do 20-damage in one turn. A Fireball for 10, a Bolt for 3, and a Shivan for the remaining 7. So fun to see.

Slivers. Had the early makings of their usual fast attack with a Metallic, Muscle and Clot Sliver, but a Fireball knocked two of them out. Ton of mana, nothing else to summon.

Fault. Maybe the best win for them all season. They were under heavy fire from Darwin, dropping most of their life total as they slowly ground away Darwin's mana supply. An early discard helped put a Rukh onto the battlefield, but it was Megrim who strung them to victory. 

Darwin. Tough loss for the perennial giant. They had a lead early but couldn't hang on as the Fault mounted their own attack, decimating both hand and mana supply. They'll look forward to the next meeting.

NLFY. A game they needed to win against a team tied for 1st place. They take out 5 lands to freeze the Hot, then sit on a few C-Kill cards to ensure nothing got by them. Two Black Vise hit the battlefield to deal all 21-damage. 

Hot. Started out slow in the mana department and never recovered. They used two Mystical Tutor's to bring lands into play, but neither one lasted more than a turn. They had some good stuff in hand to do battle, but just couldn't get to critical mana mass.

Fault. Early Jokulhaups cleared the board to elude Hecatomb's wrath while also putting a Rukh into play. The first Rukh dealt the initial 12-damage, then 5 came from a Ashes to Ashes to remove the same Rukh. A few turns later, and a Dark Ritual had given way to a Hidden Horror - Rukh combo. Game over. 

Attritionists. Got their Hecatomb in play with healthy lands (3) and all looked good for a moment - for a moment. They lacked the offense to protect their battlefield. The cards were in hand, but Jokulhaups made it onto the field faster thanks to a Sol Ring.

NLFY. Make it two in a row! They played four M-Kill cards to set up the MVP Black Vise nicely to wreak havoc on FDs large hand. NLFY looking solid in this one.

FD. Tough loss, as they compiled Urzatron despite the M-Kill yet couldn't summon their red-hand due to a lone Mountain. Would they have fared better w/o Urzatron? Maybe. Or do they need more artifacts to balance out their summoning strategy? Unclear.

Law. Now THERE'S the team I've been waiting for!! BAZINGA!! A rare 4-turn win. Let's see how it happened:

T1: Mountain, then Sol Ring
T2: Mountain, then Pandemonium
T3: Another Pandemonium after first one was countered
T4: Plateau, then Angelic Renewal and Phyrexian Dreadnought

Ooza.

Hot. No chance to compete here unless they were stocked with Counters (they weren't).

Potatoes. A four on four battle royale was set to take place until the Potatoes pulled the cord, twice. First, they wiped the field clean of all creatures via a Wrath of God once the Sliver Queen hit. Next, they wiped the field clean of all mana via Armageddon. This move ended up being critical, as the Slivers had one of every mana type in play with a hand full of goodies to play, including another Queen. The Potatoes stockpiled a few of their own lands, then won on the heels of a Mishra's Workshop.

Slivers. Were ready to dominate until all hell broke loose with the aforementioned board-clearers. They didn't draw one more land after the 'geddon, and were soon sunk.

Things. Two early Buried Alive's set the stage for a total bamboozling of the Frozen Stiffs. In fact, 41-damage was on its' way until a Meekstone showed up and ruined their party. A Dark Ritual helped summon the Living Death that won the game.

Stiffs. Tough game given mana constraints, though they had a solid hand. Will throw this loss out and will eagerly await their second match-up in the finale week of the season.

Attritionists. Weird game that they were fortunate to win. Jinxed Idol was the perfect card to douse Icy Hot - who is without many creatures. Without them, they surely would have lost.

Hot. Close loss to the last place Attritionists. The inclusion of Avizoa and Soldevi Excavation worked nicely with the theme of this squad, and I look forward to seeing them in action more.

NLFY. Textbook win against one of the fastest teams in the league. A Black Vise took care of business in this one, dealing all but two damage. They took out two lands and coasted.

Darwin. No chance to win without any mana after both of their forests were destroyed.

Drillers. Tight finish here, as they outlast the Law and win on the heels of a Power Surge that disabled their opponent. Two Lightning Bolts and an Incinerate helped.

Law. Tough loss here. They had the makings of a strong hand early on, but needed Pandemonium to put them over the top. It didn't come, and a Winds of Change didn't help either. May need more creatures in this deck.

Things. Beat the former-champ on one big counter spells. With them, they enable a Necromancy to enliven a Spirit of the Night to wreak havoc on the Potatoes with help from Hidden Horror.

Potatoes. Hard loss for them, as they had no answer to the Things. Needed more C-Kill or another Disenchant to withstand the first graveyard resurrection attack from the Things. Maybe next time.

Fault. Saw themselves in a hole quickly as they drew blood at the hands of two Metallic Slivers. One Pox, though, turned their fates around immediately as two Rukh's hit the battlefield. It was over soon thereafter.

Slivers. Four lands destroyed also destroyed their chances at a win. They had a strong hand and would have flown into a W fast, but couldn't quite get there.

Potatoes. Cruise to victory due to Frozen mana troubles.

Stiffs. Draw a mulligan, then are discard twice in first three turns due to lack of mana. Finally draw a couple of lands but are unable to play anything.

Things. What a bloodbath! Early turns saw six big blokes head into the graveyard courtesy of Buried Alive. A Living Death then brought two Polar Kraken's, two Deep Spawn's, a Spirit of the Night, a Fog Elemental and a Baron Sengir into play. The two Kraken's were dismantled, but 27-damage came pouring onto the Attritionists in the 7th turn.

Attritionists. A well played game by the Attritionists given the circumstances. They put a Hecatomb in play next to a Death Pits of Rath to firm up their defense. A Ashes to Ashes and a Diabolic Edict took care of 3 creatures, but it wasn't nearly enough.

NLFY. Strong win due to Black Vise and Demonic Hordes. Eleven damage dealt by the Vise, and the remaining ten by the Hordes.

Hot. Not enough defense in this one to fend off even one big creature. Manabarbs helped and so did Soldevi Excavations, but it wasn't enough. Close game.

Lining. The influx of mock dual-lands - Karplusan Forest specifically - paid instant dividends, enabling speed from the Slivers we haven't seen in some time, or maybe never. In 6-turns, they had seven slivers in play wreaking absolute havoc on the Drillers. Awesome.

Drillers. Struggled to summon anything namely due to colorless mana producers of the Urza variety. Thinking they help less than anticipated and may need to remove from the Driller squad.

Fault. Struggled with mana early, and had to discard as a result. What'd they discard? A Rukh Egg. Five turns later meant 20-damage for Darwin - game over.

Darwin. Needed one Fyndhorn Elf to help in the mana department, as they were stuck at 2 mana for most of the game with a hand of 3CC cards.

Drillers. Win despite seeing a nemesis card on the board; Desolation. They survived despite ending the game with 1 land. Fortunately, a Mana Flare enabled a Shivan Dragon to go out and do work. 

Attritionists. Tried to put some pressure on with Desolation, and a Jinxed Idol, but the Idol backfired on them, and the Desolation was only slightly helpful.

Potatoes. Thanks to MVP-Land Tax, the Potatoes were able to put 12 lands in play, which protected them against virtually any L-Kill efforts from NLFY. They even withstood two of their own board-clearing Armageddon's. 

NLFY. No amount of L-Kill could have won them this game, it seemed. They drew well, handled a White Knight with FA Quicksand, and dispatched other mettlesome cards with a N-Disk. Still liked what they were able to put into play though.

Darwin. Ugly win, but a win nonetheless. They were able to take down most of the Stiffs' life total with pesky 1/1 creatures.

Stiffs. Strange interaction between Scorched Ruins and Undiscovered Paradise. I like the latter, but the former just didn't help much. May need to swap that back out for Lake of the Dead, though I don't think that would have helped either. Need more data.

Slivers. A hand of dual mana producers helped put three gold slivers onto the board to wreak havoc. They took advantage of Murphy's lassitude, and forced them into a game-ending Final Fortune.

Law. Sluggish game, even after playing a Winds of Change on their first turn. They grabbed a Sol Ring, put a Lava Hounds into play with a Balduvian Horde to follow - all looked good. The Hounds were killed off, and the Horde were too late with no offense behind them.

Things. Took them a bit, but it finally ended up in a whooping. 

Hot. Not much to do with only 2-mana, but they stayed afloat as long as they could with the help of Maze of Ith.

No Landia. Scary win - they had two key cards eliminated from their hand (Diabolic Tutor & Sengir Vampire) which tilted the balance in favor of The Attritionists. Another Sengir was drawn late, and saved the day.

Attritionists. The combination of Bottomless Pit & The Rack is pretty nice, especially when a Jinxed Idol is in play like this game. Not sure what else they need, but they almost won this one.

Things. Finally, some love for Tombstone Stairwell! They helped pour the first bunch of damage on FD thanks to an early Buried Alive. Soon, though, two Kraken's, the Spirit, a Fog Elemental and the immortal Nether Spirit took home the W.

Drillers. Very, very close loss here. They needed one more Fireball to close this one up. Might need some more creature offense.

Fault. The war rages on!! The Fault stocked up their battlefield with as much heft as they could muster, throwing seven creatures into play. Finally, their 7th and final creature, a Rukh, took out the last of the Potatoes' legs. 

Potatoes. Were able to withstand the creature-onslaught from the Fault, dispatching all but one of their monsters. It wasn't enough, but it was fun :)

Icy Hot. What a game by Power Leak. They, combined with Dream Tides, completely freeze out the Stiffs. It may have taken time, but the Hot wore out their opponent with ease, watching as the Stiffs wriggled up the pressure of their mana-crunch. A late game Manabarbs sealed the fate too.

Stiffs. Their Winter Orb actually backfired in this game. Their mana froze, and then so did their creatures due to the game MVP. Without Dream Tides, the Stiffs would have won this handily. Interesting loss.

Law. So fun to see two Plateau's in the opening hand for this team. They had also drawn a Pandemonium, a Dreadnought and a few other beasts. Viashino Sandstalker looks to fit with this group also. Good win for ML.

Darwin. Amazingly, they were too slow in this game. They had enough mana, had the makings of some offense but it wasn't enough to get by the havoc wrought by Panda-Nought combo.

Slivers. Ample, and diverse, mana supply allowed them to summon anything that was drawn; Muscle, Acidic, Crystalline Slivers were joined by a Queen for a 14pt damage round to end the game. 

Hot. Were stumped due to two Scorched Ruins, and no other mana. They drew both Lotus Petals and a Sol Ring but it wasn't nearly enough to put up a fight.

Drillers. Deal 12-damage via two Bolt's and two Incinerate's, two more with a Fireball and the rest via Power Surge. Only had three mana, but it didn't matter given the low CC of their cards. Had two Horde's in-hand too.

Hot. Misplayed the new Scorched Ruins, and froze them out as a result. Need to hold onto them until there's value in playing them; Xanthic Statue or direct damage.

Fault. A nice win on the heels of some strong L-Kill. They disabled any chance that Murphy's Law would go on the offensive. Nice win.

Law. They're RIGHT there in terms of gelling as a team. It feels like they're missing a piece or two, maybe creatures, to help move faster in these games. Otherwise, they're waiting on a combination of 4-5 different cards. Too many.

Potatoes. They don't win this game without a few key players; 
- Land Tax - disabled any chance that Bottomless Pit-The Rack combo would work
- Disenchant - removed certain-death-bringer Hecatomb within two turns of being killed
- White Knight - kept the pressure on without any chance of defense from all-black opponent

Good gutsy win.

Attritionists. They had a great draw early, putting Bottomless Pit and The Rack onto the battlefield. They then threw a Hecatomb down and it looked like the end for the Potatoes. Almost had it.

Things. Start with a mulligan, then rebound to put a Hidden Horror, Spirit of the Night and Fog Elemental into play in successive turns. Night night, Darwin.

Darwin. Had a hand full of creatures with Aluren, so excitement was high, but they draw enough mana to put the attack on the Things.

Things. Drew perfectly, and played just as well. Two Buried Alive's, a Living Death, a Tormod's Crypt and then 41 damage from likely suspects to end this one swiftly on turn-6.

Slivers. The bane of their existence! Acidic Sliver would have won them the game if only Tormod's Crypt weren't involved. They could have sacrificed all Slivers twice on Living Death, if given the chance, which would have dealt 16 damage to end the game. They might need more coverage specifically for Swamp Things.

No Landia. Brilliant game by NLFY as they turn away six lands en route to a victory with 2 Sinkhole's, 2 Choking Sands and 2 Strip Mine's. They were down 20-10 by the tenth turn when MVP Royal Assassin took care of the pesky Jackal Pup. Five turns later and NLFY had wrung up 23 points of damage via the Royal, Black Knight and Sengir Vampire. Nice win.

Law. Drew ten lands in 15 turns, which is unheard of. After losing every one of their 3 red mana producers, they were stuck with four Plains and a entirely red hand. Nowhere to go but down, in defeat.

Doom. Win a battle of attrition. A Dream Tides totally stalled Darwin's early attack from a Djinn and others. Oodles of turns later, with five of their creatures dispatched, they drew the perfect card; Thelonite Druid. The Hot were still stunned from their ballsy/impatient use of Avizoa and were dealt 12-damage from a slew of enlivened trees!!! Nice work, Druid.

Hot. Dream Tides looked to be on its' way to a game MVP until one overly aggressive move left Icy Hot totally vulnerable to attack. They were hard to play with in this game, and I'm unsure what the right moves were from turn to turn. Hard to say, and while I likely made the wrong move using Avizoa to attack, there was no clear path to victory outside of waiting for a Manabarbs. Eeks.

Stiffs. Just when I started to take them apart, the Stiffs come roaring back in formidable fashion. Game-MVP, Storm Seeker, was responsible for 21 total damage. Then a host of defensive specialists - Meekstone, Winter Orb, Puppet Strings, Elvish Hunter - kept the Fault at bay. A Yawgmoth Demon landed in the 10th turn to finish off the job thanks to a Sol Ring and two Birds of Paradise. Great win against the top dog.

Fault. Totally stammered due to lack of mana. They were stuck with only 2-3 lands for much of the game and could lay no offensive thanks to 3 Rukh Eggs in play. They held off the Stiffs mounting attack for several turns but once the Demon hit the battlefield it was over.

Potatoes. Good win. The combination of Land Tax, Crusade and Icatian Town did it. Before long, there were five 2/2s attacking FD each turn.

FD. Not much in this one. Could have used a Pyroclasm in a big way. Mana Flare hurt them as it stalled out one of their turns while enabling the Icatian Town on the other end.

Things. Continue to compile the victories in the home stretch of the season, now their 4th in a row. Spirit of the Night is just too much to handle, especially for a black opponent.

NLFY. They'll only beat this team with early land destruction or significant C-Kill, though I'm dubious of the second tactic. Whoever employs their primary objective first wins, and they lagged in this one.

Sliver. Capitalized on low mana supply of opponent to hit first. Two of their initial four slivers were taken out then others were sent into battle. This was no contest.

Darwin. Stifled by lack of mana, had nothing.

Fault. The Shocker-Megrim combo is just devastating. Throw in a WoF, like in this game, and forget it. Over 20 damage was done in the final frame here thanks to those three.

Hot. Lost two key lands early and couldn't recover. Inclusion of more Lotus Petal's is the right call. This is second time in two games that Scorched Ruins has hurt them - they had two in-hand with a Xanthic Statue but just couldn't play them. Waste of 3 cards, really. Need to slot them out.

Potatoes. Retribution tastes so good!  After getting destroyed by Murphy's Law in Week 6, the Potatoes outlast them with a slim victory. The game changing performance was from Swords to Plowshares, who eliminated a Dreadnought from the game after what looked like 36-points of inevitable damage thanks to two Angelic Renewal's. An early Armageddon also helped rid the Law of 4-mana producers. They then used a slew of Mishra's Factory's to wear down the opponent dealing a total of 35-damage in this one. Nice win.

Law. What a rough loss. The early Armageddon cost them, then TWO timely Swords to Plowshares kept them from a second W against the defending champ. It was 'nought to be :)

Stiffs. Great win in this one. Here's what happened. An early Winter Orb froze the opponent while Mirri, Cat Warrior, via Worldly Tutor, started the attack. A Sol Ring and two Meekstone's came out while Yawgmoth Demon and Instill Energy, both via tutors, arrived to enhance the barrage. Undiscovered Paradise's were very useful too. Really enjoyed the synergy of the deck in this game. Maybe there's some life to this team yet!

Attritionists. Sort of sat around waiting on one more mana to begin the Autocrat/Hecatomb combo. It took too long, and also sapped them of damage that could have been done by an Underworld Dreams. The Winter Orb slowed them big time, but it's not certain they would have won otherwise. Not sure wha they need, but they need something.

Things. Great win right here. The Things withstood some C-Kill heat from the Potatoes and leveraged their Grave-Res strategy for the win, resuscitating a Deep Spawn, and then a Necrosavant, to take down the former champ. Great win that keeps them in the #1-seed.

Potatoes. Didn't have much momentum in this game despite having a hand full of control cards - Wrath of God, Armageddon, etc. They needed a bit more C-Kill to withstand the heavy onslaught, but it never came.

Drillers. Good win to distance themselves from there third pack of teams. They took advantage of a Furnace of Rath - Power Surge combo to keep Law on their heels and capitalized on their four mana to toss a Incinerate and 1-mana Fireball for 8 damage and the win.

Murphy's Law. Another game lost due to mana issues. Odd. They put literally no defense out and went down easily.

Hot. 13-turn game fit right into the Hot game plan. This came down to who drew the right card faster - Manabarbs/Drain Power for Icy Hot or Hecatomb/Sengir Autocrat for Attritionists. Hot got theirs faster, so the game shifted, but timely Boomerang also helped big time. This looked more like the team I had originally envisioned.

Attritionists. Ok, getting closer. Felt like more of a strategy here, but one that was completely reliant on the Hecatomb/Sengir Autocrat combo. Otherwise, what do they have? Well, had their first turn Underworld Dreams not been countered, they would have dealt an additional 12-damage out. Slot a meager attacker in for the Will-O-The-Wisp on the board, and that's your ballgame. Slight tinkers to follow, but getting closer.

Slivers. Raced out fast with 3 then 4 slivers, including a Muscle, to put the heat on Murphy's Law. A late Wheel of Fortune helped them take it down faster than they otherwise would have.

Law. Not enough mana to contend.

No Landia. Textbook victory against a defenseless team. Destroyed three lands thanks to two Sinkhole's and a Strip Mine, then carved out 12 points with a Black Vise as Darwin struggled to put anything in play.

Darwin. Sunk early, had no mana to summon anything and a hand full of 3-mana creature cards.

Fault. Great come-from-behind win thanks to two Megrim's, a Mind Twist and a Demonic Tutor-summoned Wheel of Fortune. All damage was dealt by the twin Megrim's. Solid to keep them in the hunt for the #1-seed.

NLFY. Had the edge early as Black Vise started to needle away, but Fault kept playing lands (even with two destroyed) to fuel the attack. Used a Dark Ritual for a Icy Manipulator which could have been a mistake, though I'm not sure they could have won this game given the Fault draw.

Potatoes. Just not scared of Darwin. They win despite having only 2 lands up until the last turn. They subside on Crusade-pumped creatures in two White Knight's and a Soul Warden. A early Reprisal to take out a Erhnam Djinnn was also helpful.

Doom. Good mana but no attack whatsoever. They had a single Djinn and two Thelonite Druid's but it was nearly enough to contend with the ever-balanced offense and defense of the Potatoes. This team needs help on the defensive end, badly.

Things. Drew perfectly to stamp another 5-turn win. Buried Alive into Living Death which, with the help of a Pandemonium on the board, enabled 28 points of damage to fly at Law. Geehoozawits.

Law. Had the victory wrapped up in one more turn. They could have played a 'Nought and a Lava Hounds to win. Tough one.

Stiffs. Win the longest game in league history and largely by design. The combination of Meekstone and Winter Orb slowed everything down to a crawl, and they were able to chip away at the Drillers with pesky's like Walking Wall and Mirri. They ran out of Demon's to play and none of them dealt any damage. The tutor's were hugely helpful, and so, again, were the slew of Undiscovered Paradise's. Really nice win and endorsement of their slow pace.

Drillers. Were SO close to taking this home. They used up a bunch of direct damage cards early to dispatch of enemy creatures, and then simply ran out. They couldn't win on the battlefield, and they didn't draw enough additional direct damage to seal the deal. Fell victim to a defense-first team in this one, but barely.

Slivers. Easy peezy lemon squeezy. No contest.

Attritionists. Played Desolation on first turn hoping to hurt the offensive firepower of the Slivers but it did virtually nothing. The Slivers pumped out land after land without issue, and took the victory with only two Slivers.

Potatoes. Ride a Crusade + Kjeldoran Outpost to victory. Outpost pumps out 5 total creatures, joined at various times by a White Knight, Soul Warden and Wild Aesthir. 

Hot. Good mana, good defensive hand for spells but not for creatures. They might need a few big walls in this deck to beef up the C-Defense.

NLFY. HUGE win for Team Jenny! Three early Sinkhole's, a Quicksand and a Demonic Tutor were instrumental in setting up a serious defensive perimeter against the league's fastest team. They destroyed three more lands, lived off of twin Icy's and gnawed away with a Black Knight. Great win.

Slivers. Six lands destroyed in this game left them stuck on an island..on a mountain :) Their good offense was beaten by a better defense in this one.

Fault. Megrim wins the MVP because of the damage multiplier, but the reason they won this game was entirely due to their M-Kill efforts. All six mountains were destroyed as the Drillers never had more than two on the board at any time. They were stranded, and likely would have won this game otherwise.

Drillers. Extremely competitive in this game despite the mana issues. They played four direct damage cards to deal 11 damage, an OK ratio (1mana to 2.75 damage), which I think is a good sign of their recent roster movement. They'll need to be competitive when lands are at a minimum. The prior roster set them up around having some mana to multiply, but it seemed to help their opponents more than them. Good changes.

Law. Viashino Sandstalker did the first twelve damage. Solid card. Enlightened Tutor helped win the game with a Pandemonium that enabled 12 more points of damage from Ball Lightning + Angelic Renewal. Good win.

Stiffs. Out of sorts. A good game to showcase why this team doesn't do enough to compete - just too much work. The first few cards played were a Birds plus Instill Energy, then three tutor's. A Thermokarst was played too (?). Tutor's brought a Puppet Strings into play to offset any attack and help stabilize the Demon's. All that to put a single Demon in play. Man.

Doom. Win despite having only two lands all game which is HIGHLY unusual. Likely lose to any other team not named The Attritionists with that kind of meager output in the mana department. Fortunately they drew an early Sol Ring who took home MVP honors in this game. Win could be crucial for their playoff position and OH YEAH i realize they only have 17 lands in this deck...?

Attritionists. Still not sure how to play with this team, though I can feel potential. Need to keep at it til the synergies start to appear. This game had options to play Jinxed Idol or a Hecatomb or a Death Pits of Rath or Bottomless Pit/The Rack. Hard to know what the goal should be each game to dictate card play..

Stiffs. Look out #1-seed!! Stiffs game plan works to perfection as they completely stifle the Things with a Winter Orb / Meekstone combo early on. They had Birds and a Sol Ring to pump out anything after that and subsided on Storm Seeker's plus a Yawgmoth Demon for the win. Nice!

Things. So here they are, the primary adversary to their resuscitation strategy. Everything, as a result, hinged on one card and it didn't come (Living Death).

Law. W-O-W. This is the fastest victory in league history, and it came as a result of a near-perfect draw by the Law. Here's how it happened:

Turn 1: Plateau, summons Enlightened Tutor for a Sol Ring
Turn 2: Plains, Sol Ring and Angelic Renewal played, suffer one mana burn
Turn 3: Mountain, Pandemonium and Phyrexian Dreadnought played for 24 damage.

Hot damn. "Bring on the playoffs," screams Murphy's Law!!!

Hot. Nothing they could do but use a counter and they didn't have it.

Fault. Destroy six lands to nullify any offense/defense from The Attritionists. That said, this game was WAY too close considering the disparity of resources. The Fault had no other means of offense, which is fine, but enabled a closer game than there should have been.

Attritionists. Stayed competitive despite the loss of lands. Some C-Kill would have helped.

Slivers. Got a great win to close out the season. The recipe was one Muscle, one Clot, one Heart and one Winged Sliver pumped by a Coat of Arms. Twenty damage hit on the 5th turn and there was nothing the Potatoes could do about it.

Potatoes. Only a Wrath of God could've helped them in this game. Or, destroying the City of Brass earlier (which was responsible for all but one card on the battlefield. So much for their hopes of a top-2 seed.

Drillers. WHAT A WILD AND UNCONVENTIONAL GAME!! The Drillers attack came from two Lightning Bolt's, a Shock, a Thunderbolt and a 6pt Fireball to win. Favor shifted back and forth as creatures were killed in the heat of battle on both sides even the rarely seen Two-Headed Giant of Foriys!! Great win to put FD in position to grab the 5-seed.

NLFY. Man, this one just got away. They had the momentum for most of the game, but lost 1-2-3 Sengir Vampire's and a Black Knight nullifying their offense. Their M-Kill efforts were tame in this one, which gave FD some life. Tough loss that slides No Landia down into the #6-seed after dropping the tiebreaker by 4 points. Absurd.

narrative.preview: Rookie squads - Big Gloves, The Anti-Dopes, Team 11 & the Glop Artists - look tough as nails. The Originals whittle down to three teams after one former great is forced to rebrand (Helloooo, Dark N Stormy). Big bruisers like Smashed Potatoes, Swamp Things and Bad Ass Fault are locked and re-loaded. Icy Hot is back due to popular demand (?). The much anticipated Season 6 is upon us. Bring the rain.

teams.writeup: Yeeeeeeeeeeeesh, that was a long season for the Hot. A 4-16 outing puts them at a 11-29 all-time record over their two seasons. A Kitty Cat Level of Ferocity with this team. They were the punching bag of the league, showing up to the battlefield with match-up apathy, just waiting for the KO. Surprisingly, their Team Captain, Manabarbs, was quite literally a game-changing card who we definitely haven't seen the last of. Penalizing the opponent for using mana was a fun new wrinkle for The League, and some teams were swallowed up by it. Put the fault on the Commish for not surrounding him with enough talent. A bevy of bounce cards looked decent on paper but cooked in play. Even a mid-season roster shake-up, thanks to noted fan Paul Kern, couldn't save them.

Ya know, this one hurts for several reasons. Teams have come and gone hoping to put old favorite Yawgmoth Demon into a winnable situation; Team 10, Frozen Stiffs. Pairing him with banjo artist Titania's Song seemed like a match made in heaven. A nucleus dependent on artifacts by...artifacts who could play D and go on offense? Hoo-rah! But the pairing was clunky, slow and put less pressure on opponents than envisioned. They had several good wins during the season, and a few painful losses, but the chemistry just isn't there. In the off-season, it's become clear that there's enormous potential for a predominantly mono-artifact outfit to be led by the banjo artist herself.

For the first time in League History, a Team was entirely replaced partway through the season. And in Season 6 this happened twice! The Anti-Dopes, built to mirror the opponent with copy spells, sat at 1-5. Most good teams have a way to put pressure on the opponent with offense and defense. This Team couldn't do either. Here's a telling comment from Week 6, 'Really have nothing going. They're not a good team, or even really a team. It's an assembly of cards that aim to thwart the opponent by prolonging the game and forcing a mirrored attack. Need an injection of new life here.' Instead, we put this Team to rest.

The Glop Artists, intending to pair Mask-Nought with a vigilance combo featuring Meekstone, was 2-5 at Week 8. The Glop Artists spent their first 7 games waiting around for the combo to hit. They were left with bloated hands and falling victim to their own sludgery with Winter Orb. The parts just didn't fit right. Pairing these two themes just didn't make sense, nor did it feel right to include Winter Orb and Meekstone with Mask-Nought anymore (Frozen Stiffs failed here, too). Alas, a major shake-up was in order for this team as well.

Mask-Nought needed more help getting one another in play, staying in play, and coming back into play if destroyed. Instead of pairing this duo with another strategic pillar, the Mist Monsters were built to focus almost entirely on supporting this combo, adding to the mystique of the Mask with low-cost creatures meant to confuse the opponent. The Monsters were up and down the rest of the way, finishing with a 6-8 record. They excelled in the Playoffs by blanking #10-Team 11 (2-0), then outlasting #7-Big Gloves (3-2) and pushing the #1-Potatoes to 5 games before losing. A strong finish. Next season will see the original rules followed and expectations are for a much faster, much stronger team.

With the Glop Artists stripped for parts, there was room for another entrant into the League. Red was added to the original green and white roster, as was a zoo of low-cost creatures to flank Lhurgoyf & Wrath of God. They went 9-4 the rest of the way, including a 7-game win streak to close out the season. This Team won 5 games with only 1 or 2 mana in play - that's insane! They rode that momentum into the playoffs, surprising Dark N Stormy in Round 1 (3-2) and then pushed #1-seeded Potatoes to 6 games before going down (4-2). One of those games against the Potatoes was record-setting, taking more turns than any game in history; 32-turns. Really one hell of a turnaround for a team that had previously gone 15-23 all-time. Welcome back from the dead, X.

A steady, reliable season for the Things who finished 8-12 in a stacked field. Their graveyard resurrection strategy continued to develop and they remained a tough out for any team that couldn't neutralize the Buried Alive + Living Death combo. A mid-season roster tweak added some speed to an otherwise methodical outfit. They were bounced in the first round of the playoffs but showed enough promise to suggest bigger things ahead in Season 7.

Oh yeah! Taking a flyer on this wacky, from left-field Team pays off as one more solid entrant into The League. Check out the commentary after their W2 loss to NLFY, 'This team is good. Still figuring out how to play them, but man are they capable of putting the heat on fast.' Yup, these hand-helpers are here to stay despite going 1-5 by Week 7. From that point on, the Gloves went 8-6 to take 7th place at 9-11. Underworld Dreams is one of the few true competitive pillars of this game, and pairing it with pieces like Teferi's Puzzle Box, Howling Mine and other draw cards was rhythmic and pulverizing. That said, their hasty pace was double-edged; they were #2 in damage given per turn, but dead last in damage taken per turn. A little more defense is needed to level them up.

Total enigma, this team. Their 9-11 record was good for 8th place this season. An average year in terms of record and offense despite an improvement over last season. Their wins were founded on the continued quick pace (4th in W-TPG; 7.67) and were buoyed by an even stronger emphasis on defense; Crumble and Winter Blast. In fact, Crumble alone helped win three games and more artifact-centric opponents are on the horizon. Green doesn't offer much in the way of staunch defensive cards, but that's where we'll start in the off-season. There's some danger in overreacting here - they were only one win away from taking home the championship last season.

A very strange season for the Fault, finishing in 6th place at 10-10 after playing by the ACTUAL rules for a change. They had a number of truly awesome comebacks worth special mention. Aside from that, though, the Fault showed some serious weaknesses on both sides of the battlefield. Offensively, their creature base was severely limited, and if Megrim didn't show, they sputtered. Defensively, they have a real tough time dealing with teams who can throw throngs of small creatures into battle.

The Smoke Makers! Alive and well and scorching faces off! An incredibly promising first official season for the direct damage heavers built on the heels of Fire Drilled's legacy had them finish in 4th place at 12-8, narrowly missing the #3 seed. Stormy was Top 3 in both U-DPI (0.115) and DPT (2.01). The Team doubled-down on low-cost flamethrowers, like Lightning Bolt, Incinerate and Chain Lightning, to keep competitive with a increasingly nimble league. Their aggressive style combined with low-cost spells had them finishing most games with almost half as many cards in-hand as opponents. To improve, the Smoke Makers could use a little more creature defense.

Back to their winning ways, the Slivers closed down the regular season at 14-6 to take 2nd place. Their 0.159 U-DPI was best in the league and good for 2nd All-Time. Their offense was #1 in the league (2.89 DPT) and good for #2 All-Time. The Slivers were ferocious this season and it felt like the championship was theirs to lose. They also had the 4th ranked defense, and the fastest win-pace in the league at 7.29 turns per win. They ran a balanced attack this year and once again benefited from the variety of mana producers on their team. On defense, nothing hurt the Slivers more than artifacts. Their post-season demise echoed a familiar refrain: 'What a disappointing finish. Seeing the Vise on the first turn was nearly doomsday - they aren't equipped to fend off the artifacts and were too slow out the gate to overcompensate.'

The defending champ had their best regular season in two years, nailing down 3rd place on a 13-7 record. Their U-DPI rose from 0.074 last season to 0.085, namely on a stronger defensive outing. No Landia held teams to only 1.18 DPT (2nd best). Further research showed that NFLY does a heck of a job destroying opposing lands. No Landia's playoff outing was solid, defending the crown well, beating fellow-land-crusher Bad Ass Fault in five (3-2), then handling the high-powered Slivers (4-2) before falling flat against the Potatoes (4-0). It was an odd finale. NLFY looked like they were completely out of gas and maybe they should have been after winning the 'ship in 7 last year.

Reclaim the crown with a demonstrative sweep against rival #3-NLFY in the finale. What a way to take it home! They rode nice momentum after dispatching #5-Extinction in the semi-finals (4-2), and outlasting the enigmatic #11-Mist Monsters in the opening round (3-2). Capturing the #1-seed with a 15-5 record was their first ever. How'd they do it? Defense. The average damage taken per turn across the league was 1.78. The Potatoes, by comparison, allowed only 0.77 damage per turn. That's good for 3rd All-Time. From a pace perspective, they took their time, using over 11 turns per game to reach an outcome. The key to their victories was not only land destruction (Armageddon, Cleansing, Balance) but the ability to benefit from it via Land Tax / Zuran Orb. Time for the Potatoes to sit back, simmer and smile at their two championships in 3 years.

The Anti-Dopes, built to mirror the opponent with copy spells, sat at 1-5 before being entirely replaced by the Mist Monsters. For the first time in League History, a Team was entirely replaced partway through the season. The Mist Monsters stepped in to give the Mask-Nought combo the focused support it always needed, finishing 6-8 the rest of the way and making a strong run in the playoffs.

The Glop Artists were 2-5 at Week 8 before being overhauled. Red was added to the original green and white roster as was a zoo of low-cost creatures. Extinction went 9-4 the rest of the way, including a 7-game win streak to close out the season. A stunning turnaround from a team that had been 15-23 all-time.

game_commentary.commentary:
Hot. WHOA!!! After taking some heat via a pumped STDevils, the Hot steal Game 1 on the hands of two Control Magic for a Hidden Horror and SotNight! The Things are dumbfounded, and so are the Hot!!!

Things. Ouch. The two Control Magic were game-changing, but so was the Mana Leak on Tombstone Stairwell which had big implications (0 creatures in Hot graveyard 5 in Things).

Monsters. What a doozie. Twenty long turns to close this one out. It eventually got to a standstill with a crowded battlefield of powderpuffs, but the difference maker were the 3 Maze of Ith that kept an enlivened Icy, Su-Chi and Yawggy out of play. They outlasted Team 11 in a battle of attrition.

Team 11. Ugly game. Threw Titania's Song in early to try and tip the balance while also keeping the Monsters from using the Mask. Good call, but were stifled by the three Maze of Ith. Without them, this one is over much sooner.

Potatoes. Very very nice win. Doubling down on Land Tax - Zuran Orb - Mana Destruction gave a path to victory in this game. They simply outlasted the Hot and were able to stockpile 25 extra life as the game went on. Kjeldoran Outpost helped, and so did Mishra's Factory. Good win. Like this new look.

Hot. Lose six lands in this game. No Mountain lands late hurt them but it SO FUN to see Invoke Prejudice out there! Put one Manabarbs out there and this game would look much different. Power Surge and muchos Boomerang cards fit nicely, and so do the Maze of Ith cards. Need some attack in here though.

Stormy. Win via creature base - a new tactic for them. A Ball Lightning for 12 damage, thanks to Furnace of Rath, and then a Thunderbolt w/ Fork ended things for another 12 damage. Solid.

Anti. It's early :) no idea what their north star is yet but I like the construct of stealing any/everything in play - just don't have enough defense in yet. Hand full of 4-5CC cards kept them out of the game. Stasis sat there too - unsure how to use it yet.

Monsters. Whoa baby! A second turn Relic Barrier stifles the early attack from 11 (Su-Chi coming otherwise). They then put a Mask out, then a Nought, and proceed to deal 24-damage over the next two turns. Two Maze of Ith, which stifled 11 in Game 1, were on the board to provide any necessary defense once again. Great win to advance!

Team 11. Disappointing win but the Monsters drew their deadly combo and they were kaputzky afterward.
